steps.title: Identify both perspectives in the passage
steps.content: The passage presents two opposing views of the same house. The parents see it negatively: "not really a pretty house," "rather inconvenient," "no shelves," ironwork "like an architect's nightmare." The children see it positively: after two years in London with no countryside escape, it seemed "a sort of Fairy Palace set down in an Earthly Paradise." The word "But" signals the shift between the two views. The main idea must capture this contrast accurately.

steps.title: Eliminate each wrong choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — Although their parents believe the house has several drawbacks, the children are enchanted by it.: "drawbacks" matches the parents' complaints and "enchanted" matches the children's "Fairy Palace" response — both accurate to the text. ✓
  • Choice B — The children don't like the house nearly as much as their parents do.: reverses the passage; the parents are the ones dissatisfied, not the children. ✗
  • Choice C — Each member of the family admires a different characteristic of the house.: the parents express no admiration; they criticize it throughout. ✗
  • Choice D — The house is beautiful and well built, but the children miss their old home in London.: the passage explicitly says it is "not really a pretty house," and the children are delighted to be out of London. ✗
That matches Choice A — Although their parents believe the house has several drawbacks, the children are enchanted by it..
